Methodology & interpretation — revenue scope, the SEC reconciliation, and how the margin is calculated

1 · Revenue (the denominator)

Every figure is built from Net Patient Revenue on the hospital cost report (CMS Form 2552, Worksheet G-3) — gross patient charges less contractual allowances and discounts. It is an all-payer, hospital-facility number. It excludes: (1) non-hospital operations a system owns — freestanding ambulatory surgery centers & ERs, urgent care, imaging, home health, retail pharmacy, and any insurance/health-plan premiums; and (2) other operating revenue — grants, 340B, cafeteria, parking, investment/JV equity income. Physician professional (Part B) revenue is structure-dependent: when employed physicians sit in a separate group / foundation entity (the common arrangement) their professional fees are billed under the physician fee schedule and are not on the hospital's G-3; when a hospital employs physicians within its own legal entity, some professional revenue can flow into G-3 NPR. (Either way the Medicare cost report strips the physician professional-component cost via the provider/professional split.) For facility-heavy operators like HCA it's immaterial; for heavily-employed integrated systems it can lift the ratio toward reported revenue. So CMS revenue runs below a system's consolidated top line.

2 · Reconciling CMS to reported results (for-profit SEC filers)

For HCA, Tenet, and UHS the table above bridges CMS to the 10-K. Two gaps, and they run in opposite directions:

  • Revenue: SEC > CMS (HCA FY2023: +$8.8B). This is predominantly non-hospital revenue — HCA's large freestanding-ER and ambulatory-surgery network, physician services, and other operating revenue. It is not "missing hospitals": HCA's ~185 licensed campuses file as ~162 cost reports because second campuses and rehab/psych units consolidate into a co-located flagship's single CCN (a 1,785-bed “Methodist Hospital” in San Antonio aggregates several campuses; HCA Florida Largo includes Largo West; Wesley includes Woodlawn; Henrico Doctors includes Parham & Retreat). That campus revenue is already inside the CMS figure — the AHRQ hospital count simply splits campuses that CMS consolidates.
  • Operating income: CMS > SEC (HCA FY2023: +$4.8B). The hospital P&L excludes corporate / home-office overhead, G&A, and management fees booked above the hospital. Crucially, depreciation and interest ARE captured in the CMS operating expense — so this is a genuine corporate-overhead gap, not a depreciation/EBITDA artifact.

3 · The margin, and why it skews in two directions

Operating margin = (Net Patient Revenue − Total Operating Expense) ÷ Net Patient Revenue, at the individual-hospital level. The direction of the skew depends on the operator:

  • For-profit chains (HCA, Tenet, UHS): corporate cost sits above the hospital, so the CMS margin runs higher than the consolidated GAAP operating margin. For HCA FY2023 the ladder is CMS 25.7% > SEC EBITDA 19.6% > SEC operating 14.8% — the ~$5–7B corporate layer is why CMS sits even above EBITDA.
  • Academic / research systems: the hospital absorbs teaching and research cost that patient revenue doesn't fund, so the CMS margin runs far lower (often deeply negative) than reported.

Bottom line: read CMS operating margin as a hospital patient-services margin for cross-hospital and cross-market comparison — not as a system's reported profitability. The SEC table reconciles the two for the for-profit filers.

4 · Data quality

Implausible margins from CMS cost-report errors (negative or near-zero revenue denominators — e.g. a hospital whose NPR rounds to $0) are nulled at the source (kept within a ±75% band); impossible bed counts (>3,000) are nulled; duplicate partial-year / ownership-change filings are de-duped to the single longest fiscal period. System membership is year-matched to AHRQ health-system rosters (merger predecessors folded into the current system_id); the CMS "Final" file lags ~1–2 years, so the most recent year is the least complete.

Ranking

Scale vs. profitability

bubble = staffed beds
Quadrants split at peer medians · point sized by beds
p25 / median / p75 lines are computed across the selected system scope. Rate metrics (margins, occupancy) are directly comparable; dollar metrics scale with system size and show their full range. Implausible margins (CMS cost-report data errors in small systems — negative or near-zero revenue denominators) are dropped at the source; rate metrics additionally guard residual outliers. Switch to Featured or Top-X to focus on large systems.

Hospitals in state

State summary

One row per state (selected year). CR5 = combined share of the 5 largest health systems (by staffed beds) among all hospitals in the state — a market-concentration measure. Top-5 margin and all-hospital margin are NPR-weighted operating (patient-services) margins. Click any header to sort.
